title: The Twelfth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Sermon: "The Twelfth Sunday after Pentecost" | Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver
Date: August 15, 2021 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon falls on the Twelfth Sunday after Pentecost, placing the church in Ordinary Time following Pentecost and before Advent. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date would have included 1 Kings 2:10-12, 3:3-14 (Solomon's request for wisdom), Psalm 111, Ephesians 5:15-20, and John 6:51-58 (the discourse on Jesus as the bread of life). The thematic emphasis would have centered on wisdom, spiritual discernment, and the Eucharistic mystery of Christ's body and blood.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church in 2021 was navigating post-pandemic reopening and vaccine distribution discussions, with parishes across the country adjusting worship protocols. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Kee (Anita Bradshaw), was managing the reintegration of in-person worship after COVID-19 lockdowns. No major General Convention actions or bishop elections were occurring at this specific moment, though the broader church continued addressing racial justice and LGBTQ+ inclusion initiatives that had gained momentum since 2018.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver in mid-August 2021 was experiencing intense heat and drought conditions affecting the region throughout the summer. The week before this sermon saw continued reporting on Colorado's water crisis and wildfire threat, with the state in severe drought status. Saint John's Cathedral, located in downtown Denver's Capitol Hill neighborhood, would have been serving a congregation navigating return-to-office decisions, as many Colorado businesses were adjusting to post-vaccine workplace policies.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Biden administration faced significant turmoil with the chaotic withdrawal from Afghanistan accelerating—on August 15, 2021, the Taliban completed their takeover of Kabul as the final U.S. military operations concluded. The nation was gripped by disturbing images of desperate Afghans attempting to flee Kabul airport, creating a domestic political crisis for the new administration. Meanwhile, COVID-19 delta variant cases were surging across the United States despite vaccination efforts, and debates over mask mandates and school reopening were intensifying as the fall academic year approached.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Taliban's complete military victory and seizure of Afghanistan's capital Kabul on August 15 was the dominant international story, marking the end of the 20-year U.S.-led war and raising immediate humanitarian concerns for Afghan civilians and religious minorities. Taliban fighters were already engaged in reprisal killings and consolidating control over major cities. In Haiti, the country was reeling from a devastating 7.2 magnitude earthquake that struck on August 14, killing over 2,200 people and prompting international humanitarian response.

CULTURAL MOMENT

August 2021 reflected a nation at an inflection point—partially reopened but anxious, celebrating vaccination progress while confronting the delta surge and Afghanistan's sudden fall. The mood was one of uncertainty: Americans were cautiously returning to offices, schools were reopening amid mask debates, and the Afghanistan withdrawal symbolized geopolitical retrenchment that felt destabilizing. Denver's cultural life was gradually recovering, though pandemic wariness remained; this was also Colorado Rockies season, and the team was mid-season in a rebuilding year.
